message.content:
Remote Access Software (T1219) involves adversaries leveraging legitimate remote desktop and support tools like TeamViewer, AnyDesk, and LogMeIn to establish command and control (C2) channels within target networks. These tools, often permitted by application controls, can be used post-compromise for redundant access or interactive remote sessions. Adversaries may install these tools to maintain persistence, often creating Windows Services during installation. 

Common exploitation methods include using these tools as part of malware to establish reverse connections or back-connects to adversary-controlled systems. Notable groups such as Carbanak, Cobalt Group, and Sandworm Team have utilized these tools for remote access and lateral movement. For instance, Carbanak used AmmyyAdmin and TeamViewer, while Cobalt Group employed Ammyy Admin to maintain access if other modules failed.

The impact of this technique includes unauthorized remote control, data exfiltration, and potential disruption of services. Mitigation strategies involve strict application control policies, monitoring for unusual remote access tool installations, and employing network segmentation to limit the spread of compromised systems. Regular audits and endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions can help identify and block unauthorized remote access attempts.
